A tenth-century Persian philosopher who worked to reconcile Greek philosophy with Islamic theology, he was active in Nishapur, Bukhara, and Baghdad. His major work al-I'lam bi-Manaqib al-Islam (Proclamation of the Merits of Islam) argued that Islamic revelation completed and perfected the philosophical traditions of the Greeks. He was an important figure in the transmission of Neoplatonic ideas within the Islamic intellectual tradition.

A tenth-century Arabic prose writer and philosopher based in Baghdad, he is widely regarded as one of the greatest stylists of classical Arabic prose. His works al-Imta' wa-l-Mu'anasa (Enjoyment and Conviviality) and al-Muqabasat (Conversations) record philosophical and literary discussions at the courts of Buyid-era officials, preserving a vivid portrait of intellectual life in tenth-century Baghdad. Despite his literary brilliance, he lived in poverty and obscurity for much of his life.

A tenth- and eleventh-century Persian philosopher, historian, and ethicist who served the Buyid dynasty, he is best known for his ethical treatise Tahdhib al-Akhlaq (Refinement of Character), which synthesised Greek, Persian, and Islamic moral thought. His universal history Tajarib al-Umam (Experiences of the Nations) adopted an analytical approach that emphasised causation over mere chronology. He was a pioneering figure in Islamic philosophical ethics and influenced later thinkers including al-Ghazali.
